module_title: Episode 12: Tequila & Mezcal — Heart of the Agave
title: Heart of the Agave: A Sacred Plant Made Spirit
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain why agave spirits matter and how this episode studies them.
Subject: Episode introduction | Run time: about 3 minutes

[WELCOME] Welcome back to Better Vice Club, Season 2, The Oldest Toast. This is Anthony McDonald. We have followed grain, grape, and cane. Today we reach the agave, a desert plant that Mexico has lived with for thousands of years, and the source of tequila and mezcal.

[HOOK] The honest note first. This is a history and economics class, not a bar, and we will be straight about alcohol's harms. Now the hook. Agave is not like the other plants in this season. Beer's grain, wine's grape, and rum's cane all want comfort, good soil and steady water. Agave wants the opposite. It thrives on hardship, poor soil, drought, and heat, and the harder its life, the richer its sugar. Agave is the most extreme example of the idea we have tracked all season: that struggle makes flavor.

[TEACH] We read agave spirits through four lenses. Geography asks why agave is a stress specialist, why highland and lowland Jalisco make different tequila, and why some agave species take 30 years to grow. Social studies tells how agave was sacred to the Aztecs, how Spanish colonizers burned the books that held that knowledge, and how a sacred plant became a global commodity. Economics asks who profits from a sixty-dollar bottle, why the person who harvests the agave earns the least, and how small mezcal makers fight back. English language arts teaches how to read an Aztec painted book, and how to read a tequila label that sells Indigenous heritage. Each lesson is short and ends with a quick question.

[REMEMBER] Hold this. Agave is a stress specialist, a plant that turns hardship into sweetness, and it carries a sacred history that was nearly erased. To study tequila and mezcal honestly is to hold the craft, the culture, and the cost together.

[CHECK] Quick check. How is agave different from grain, grape, and cane in what it needs to grow? [Beat] Those crops want comfort, good soil and water, but agave thrives on hardship, poor soil, drought, and heat, which makes its sugar richer.

## Key Takeaways
- Agave is a desert succulent native to the Americas and the source of tequila and mezcal.
- Unlike grain, grape, or cane, agave is a stress specialist that makes richer sugar under hardship.
- This episode is educational and honest about alcohol's harms; it is not an endorsement of drinking.
- Better Vice Club reads agave spirits through geography, social studies, economics, and English language arts.

## Sources
- Valenzuela-Zapata, A. G., & Nabhan, G. P. (2004). Tequila! A natural and cultural history. University of Arizona Press.

module_title: Episode 12: Tequila & Mezcal — Heart of the Agave
title: The Stress Specialist
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain what agave needs to grow and why hardship makes it sweeter.
Subject: Geography | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we opened the agave episode. Two quick questions.
One: how is agave different from grain, grape, and cane? [Beat] It thrives on hardship rather than comfort (Valenzuela-Zapata & Nabhan, 2004).
Two: what does that hardship do to its sugar? [Beat] It makes the sugar richer.

[HOOK] Almost every crop a farmer grows wants the same things: rich soil, plenty of water, easy conditions. Agave is the rebel. Give it an easy life and you get a boring plant. Make its life hard, and it rewards you. Agave turns suffering into sweetness, and that is the whole secret of these spirits.

[TEACH] Agave is a succulent, a desert plant, and the genus has more than 200 species native to the Americas (Valenzuela-Zapata & Nabhan, 2004). Most species grow best high up, between about 3,000 and 7,500 feet, where the air is thin and growth is slow. They want volcanic, well-drained soil that is low in organic matter, only 15 to 40 inches of rain a year, and big swings in temperature between day and night and across seasons. Where another plant would struggle, agave stores energy.
Here is the mechanism. When a plant faces stress, drought, poor soil, cold nights, it slows down and concentrates its resources, packing more sugar into its core to survive. Agave is a stress specialist, so the harder its environment within its range, the denser and more complex its sugar becomes (Valenzuela-Zapata & Nabhan, 2004). And sugar is what distillers turn into spirit. So a tough mountain agave makes a richer, more complex tequila or mezcal than a pampered one ever could.
This is the most extreme version of terroir we have seen all season. With wine and whiskey, a little stress helped. With agave, stress is the entire point.

[PICTURE] Picture a steep, rocky volcanic hillside, dry and sun-blasted, with spiky blue-green agaves spread across it. Nothing else wants to grow there. The agave loves it, slowly swelling its heart with concentrated sugar over years of hardship. The worse the ground looks to us, the better the spirit it will make.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: agave is a stress specialist that grows best in high, dry, volcanic, low-nutrient ground, and the harder its conditions within its range, the more concentrated and complex its sugar becomes (Valenzuela-Zapata & Nabhan, 2004). Hardship is the flavor.

[CHECK] Quick check. Why does a tough environment make agave better for spirits? [Beat] Stress makes the agave concentrate more, denser, more complex sugar in its core, and that sugar becomes the spirit (Valenzuela-Zapata & Nabhan, 2004).

## Key Takeaways
- Agave is a desert succulent with more than 200 species native to the Americas (Valenzuela-Zapata & Nabhan, 2004).
- It grows best high up in volcanic, well-drained, low-nutrient soil with little rain and big temperature swings.
- As a stress specialist, agave concentrates richer, more complex sugar the harder its conditions are.
- That makes agave the most extreme example of terroir in the whole season.

## Sources
- Valenzuela-Zapata, A. G., & Nabhan, G. P. (2004). Tequila! A natural and cultural history. University of Arizona Press.

module_title: Episode 12: Tequila & Mezcal — Heart of the Agave
title: Highland and Lowland
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain how the same agave makes different tequila in highland and lowland Jalisco.
Subject: Geography | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we met the stress specialist. Two quick questions.
One: what kind of soil does agave want? [Beat] Volcanic, well-drained, low in nutrients (Valenzuela-Zapata & Nabhan, 2004).
Two: what does stress do to agave's sugar? [Beat] It concentrates and complicates it (Valenzuela-Zapata & Nabhan, 2004).

[HOOK] Tequila has rules about where it can be made, and inside those rules, one Mexican state grows nearly all of it. But even within that state, the exact same agave plant makes two very different tequilas, depending on whether it grew up high or down low.

[TEACH] First, the rules. Tequila has a Denominacion de Origen, a legal designation that says tequila can only be made in certain regions, with Jalisco at the center, and only from one plant, the blue Weber agave (Bowen, 2015). That is geography written into law, which we will dig into in the economics lessons.
Now the split inside Jalisco. Up in the highlands, in Los Altos, the agave grows at 6,500 to 7,500 feet in cool red volcanic clay, with big temperature swings (Valenzuela-Zapata & Nabhan, 2004). That cool, stressful, high life builds extra sugar, and the tequila comes out floral and sweet. Down in the Tequila Valley lowlands, at 3,500 to 5,000 feet, the agave grows in warm dark volcanic loam with less temperature variation, and the tequila comes out earthier, more herbal and vegetal.
Same plant, same species, two flavors, and the only difference is elevation, soil, and temperature. This is exactly the terroir lesson from wine's limestone and whiskey's water, now playing out across one Mexican state. The land writes itself into the bottle.

[PICTURE] Look at the map on this lesson. Picture two zones in Jalisco. Up high, cool red clay hills where the agave turns sweet and floral. Down low, warm dark valley soil where it turns earthy and herbal. One agave, two homes, two spirits.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: tequila must be made from blue Weber agave in designated regions, and within Jalisco the highland agave of Los Altos makes floral, sweet tequila while the lowland agave of the Tequila Valley makes earthier, herbal tequila (Bowen, 2015; Valenzuela-Zapata & Nabhan, 2004). Elevation is flavor.

[CHECK] Quick check. Why does highland Jalisco tequila taste different from lowland tequila if both use the same agave? [Beat] The highland agave grows higher, cooler, and more stressed, making it sweeter and floral, while the warmer lowland makes it earthier and herbal (Valenzuela-Zapata & Nabhan, 2004).

## Key Takeaways
- Tequila has a Denominacion de Origen limiting it to designated regions, centered on Jalisco, and to blue Weber agave (Bowen, 2015).
- Highland Los Altos agave grows high, cool, and stressed in red clay, giving floral, sweet tequila.
- Lowland Tequila Valley agave grows warmer in dark loam, giving earthier, herbal tequila.
- The same agave species becomes two spirits, so elevation and soil are written into the bottle.

## Sources
- Bowen, S. (2015). Divided spirits: Tequila, mezcal, and the politics of production. University of California Press.
- Valenzuela-Zapata, A. G., & Nabhan, G. P. (2004). Tequila! A natural and cultural history. University of Arizona Press.

module_title: Episode 12: Tequila & Mezcal — Heart of the Agave
title: Agave and Time
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain why agave's long growth creates an ecological risk for wild species.
Subject: Geography | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we split highland and lowland Jalisco. Two quick questions.
One: what one agave species is tequila made from? [Beat] Blue Weber agave (Bowen, 2015).
Two: which makes sweeter, floral tequila, highland or lowland? [Beat] Highland, the cooler and more stressed Los Altos (Valenzuela-Zapata & Nabhan, 2004).

[HOOK] Most crops are ready in a season. Agave thinks in decades. Some of the agaves used for mezcal take longer to mature than a person takes to finish school, and that slow clock has become a serious ecological problem.

[TEACH] Agave does not grow fast, and different species take very different lengths of time (Torres et al., 2015). Blue Weber, the tequila agave, takes about 6 to 8 years. Espadin, the workhorse of mezcal, takes 7 to 10. But the prized wild species take far longer. Tobala can take 12 to 20 years. And Tepeztate can take 25 to 35 years to mature, longer than many of the people harvesting it have been alive.
Here is the ecological math, and it is unforgiving. An agave only makes seeds when it flowers, near the end of its long life. But to make spirit, the plant is harvested just before it flowers, when its sugar peaks. So harvesting a wild agave usually means it never reproduces. If a 30-year-old Tepeztate is cut down faster than new ones can grow and flower, the species declines (Torres et al., 2015). Rising global demand for wild-agave mezcal is now pushing several species, including Tepeztate, toward being threatened or vulnerable.
This is the direct cost of unsustainable production. A spirit that takes thirty years to grow cannot be made quickly without emptying the hillsides. The slow clock of agave is both its gift, all that concentrated sugar, and its danger.

[PICTURE] Picture a single Tepeztate agave, planted when someone's grandparent was young, finally tall enough to harvest after three decades. Now picture a hillside of them cut down in one demand-driven season, with no seeds left behind. The math does not add up, and the species thins.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: agave matures slowly, from about 6 years for blue Weber to 25 to 35 years for wild Tepeztate, and because the plant is harvested just before it flowers, overharvesting wild species faster than they can reproduce pushes them toward extinction (Torres et al., 2015). Time is the hidden cost.

[CHECK] Quick check. Why does harvesting wild agave threaten the species? [Beat] Agave only seeds when it flowers, but it is harvested just before flowering, so cutting it faster than it can reproduce makes the species decline (Torres et al., 2015).

## Key Takeaways
- Agave matures slowly: about 6 to 8 years for blue Weber, but 25 to 35 years for wild Tepeztate (Torres et al., 2015).
- Agave only makes seeds when it flowers, near the end of its life, but it is harvested just before flowering.
- So harvesting wild agave usually stops it from reproducing, and overharvesting makes species decline.
- Rising demand for wild-agave mezcal is pushing several species toward being threatened.

## Sources
- Torres, I., Casas, A., Vega, E., Martinez-Ramos, M., & Delgado-Lemus, A. (2015). Population dynamics and sustainable management of mescal agaves in central Mexico. Economic Botany, 69(1), 26-41.
- Bowen, S. (2015). Divided spirits: Tequila, mezcal, and the politics of production. University of California Press.

module_title: Episode 12: Tequila & Mezcal — Heart of the Agave
title: The Sacred Plant
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain how agave was sacred to the Aztecs and how they governed its fermented drink.
Subject: Social Studies | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we talked about agave and time. Two quick questions.
One: why does harvesting wild agave threaten the species? [Beat] Agave only makes seeds when it flowers, but it is harvested just before flowering, so cutting it faster than it can reproduce makes the species decline (Torres et al., 2015).
Two: roughly how long does wild Tepeztate take to mature? [Beat] About 25 to 35 years, longer than many of the people harvesting it have been alive (Torres et al., 2015).

[HOOK] Long before tequila, before mezcal, before any still ever touched this plant, people in Mexico were growing agave. The relationship is so old that it makes most farming traditions look young. And the drink they made from it was not a casual thing. It was sacred, it had a goddess, and the law decided exactly who was allowed to touch it.

[TEACH] Start with how deep this goes. There is evidence of agave cultivation in Mexico from around 10,000 BCE, which makes it one of the oldest agricultural relationships in the country (Bruman, 2000). People did not just stumble onto agave. They lived alongside it for thousands of years.
Out of that long relationship came pulque, the fermented sap of the agave plant. Not distilled, just fermented, a mildly alcoholic drink, and the records of it in ceremonial and daily life run from about 2000 to 500 BCE (Bruman, 2000). So this is not a drink that showed up late. It was woven into life for a very long time.
And it was holy. Pulque was tied to the goddess Mayahuel, the deity of the agave plant herself (Bruman, 2000). When a drink has a goddess, you are not talking about something people treated lightly. You are talking about something at the center of belief.
Now the part I want you to really sit with. The Aztecs did not just have pulque, they governed it, carefully. Their law set who was allowed to drink. Elders could. Nursing mothers could, in limited amounts. Warriors could, after battle. Priests could, in ritual. And the general population only at certain festivals (Bruman, 2000). Everyone else, the rest of the time, was not supposed to drink at all.
Enforcement was serious. Public drunkenness outside those permitted contexts was met with public shaming, and for repeat offenders, the punishment was death (Bruman, 2000).
Here is the point that matters. That is a more differentiated system than most modern alcohol law. Think about it. Our rules mostly come down to one line: how old are you. The Aztec system asked who you were, what your role was, and what the occasion was. It governed a powerful substance by social role and context, not by a blanket ban and not by a blanket allowance.
That should sound familiar. Back in Season 1 we saw the same logic in kava protocols in the Pacific and in guayusa community practices. Different plants, different parts of the world, same idea. Indigenous societies built thoughtful frameworks for governing psychoactive substances, embedding them inside the social structure instead of leaving them to the market or banning them outright.

[PICTURE] Picture a festival night in the Aztec world. The agave goddess Mayahuel is honored, and pulque is poured, but only for the people the law allows: an elder, a warrior just back from battle, a priest in ritual. A young person reaching for a cup outside that circle is stopped, because the rule is not about thirst, it is about role. The drink belongs to a place in the social order, not to everyone.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: agave has been cultivated in Mexico since around 10,000 BCE, its fermented sap pulque was sacred and tied to the goddess Mayahuel, and Aztec law governed who could drink it by social role and occasion, a more differentiated system than most modern alcohol law (Bruman, 2000).

[CHECK] Quick check. How did Aztec pulque law decide who could drink? [Beat] By social role and occasion, not by a single rule. Elders, nursing mothers, warriors after battle, and priests in ritual were permitted, with the general population allowed only at certain festivals, and public drunkenness outside those contexts was punished (Bruman, 2000).

## Key Takeaways
- Agave has been cultivated in Mexico since around 10,000 BCE, one of the country's oldest agricultural relationships (Bruman, 2000).
- Pulque, the fermented agave sap, appears in ceremonial and daily life from about 2000 to 500 BCE and was sacred, tied to the goddess Mayahuel (Bruman, 2000).
- Aztec law governed who could drink pulque by social role: elders, nursing mothers, warriors after battle, priests in ritual, and the general population only at festivals (Bruman, 2000).
- This was more differentiated than most modern alcohol law, showing Indigenous societies governed powerful substances by role and context, like Season 1's kava and guayusa protocols.

## Sources
- Bruman, H. J. (2000). Alcohol in ancient Mexico. University of Utah Press.
- Torres, I., Casas, A., Vega, E., Martinez-Ramos, M., & Delgado-Lemus, A. (2015). Population dynamics and sustainable management of mescal agaves in central Mexico. Economic Botany, 69(1), 26-41.

module_title: Episode 12: Tequila & Mezcal — Heart of the Agave
title: Burning the Books
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain what Mesoamerican codices were and why their destruction was a colonial strategy.
Subject: Social Studies | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we talked about the sacred plant. Two quick questions.
One: which goddess was pulque tied to? [Beat] Mayahuel, the deity of the agave plant (Bruman, 2000).
Two: how did Aztec pulque law decide who could drink? [Beat] By social role and occasion, with elders, nursing mothers, warriors after battle, and priests in ritual permitted, and the general population allowed only at festivals (Bruman, 2000).

[HOOK] Imagine a whole library of a civilization's history, its religion, its star charts, its calendars, all painted by hand. Now imagine almost all of it set on fire on purpose. That is close to what happened to the books of Mesoamerica. And the burning was not random. It was a strategy.

[TEACH] First, what were these books. Mesoamerican codices were painted books, made on deer hide or on bark paper, and they recorded history, religion, astronomy, tribute, calendars, and daily life (Leon-Portilla, 1992). They did not use an alphabet like European writing. They used a sophisticated visual language, telling stories and holding data through images, color, and arrangement on the page (Leon-Portilla, 1992). Reading one was a skill of its own, a different kind of literacy, not a lesser one.
Now the scale of what was lost, and it is hard to hear. Thousands of codices existed before contact with the Spanish. Today, fewer than 20 pre-Columbian examples survive (Leon-Portilla, 1992). Fewer than twenty, out of thousands. One of the major burnings was Bishop Diego de Landa's auto-da-fe in Yucatan in 1562, where thousands of Maya texts were destroyed in a single campaign (Leon-Portilla, 1992).
Why did the friars do it. By their own words, they called the codices "works of the devil" (Leon-Portilla, 1992). To them, burning the books was framed as a religious duty, a way to wipe out what they saw as superstition.
But the historian Miguel Leon-Portilla, who spent his career recovering pre-Columbian literature, makes a sharper argument about what was really going on. His point is that a knowledge system holds up a people's identity, and that destroying that knowledge system speeds up cultural assimilation (Leon-Portilla, 1992). In other words, the burning was not a side effect of conquest. It was inseparable from colonial domination. You cannot fully control a people while their record of who they are stays intact.
And that same logic did not stop at the books. It also drove the suppression of Indigenous languages and the banning of ceremonies, including the pulque rituals we talked about last time (Leon-Portilla, 1992). Burn the books, silence the language, ban the ceremony. Three different actions, one strategy. Erase the knowledge, and the people are easier to absorb.
That is why this lesson sits next to the agave story instead of off in its own corner. The plant that was sacred, the goddess who carried it, the rituals that surrounded it, all of that lived in the codices and in the ceremonies. When the books burned, a piece of the agave's own history burned with them.

[PICTURE] Look at the codex shown in this lesson. Picture a folded strip of bark paper or deer hide, covered edge to edge in bright painted figures, no alphabet anywhere, every image carrying meaning. Now picture a fire in Yucatan in 1562, and thousands of pages like this one going up in smoke, with only a handful of books in the whole world surviving to be read today.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: Mesoamerican codices were painted books recording history, religion, astronomy, and daily life in a visual language, thousands existed before contact but fewer than 20 pre-Columbian ones survive, and Leon-Portilla argues their destruction was inseparable from colonial domination because erasing a knowledge system speeds cultural assimilation (Leon-Portilla, 1992).

[CHECK] Quick check. What did Leon-Portilla argue about destroying a knowledge system? [Beat] That a knowledge system holds up a people's cultural identity, so destroying it speeds up assimilation, which made codex burning inseparable from colonial domination, the same logic that suppressed Indigenous languages and banned ceremonies (Leon-Portilla, 1992).

## Key Takeaways
- Mesoamerican codices were painted books on deer hide or bark paper recording history, religion, astronomy, tribute, calendars, and daily life in a sophisticated visual language (Leon-Portilla, 1992).
- Thousands existed before contact, but fewer than 20 pre-Columbian examples survive; Bishop Diego de Landa's 1562 auto-da-fe in Yucatan destroyed thousands of Maya texts (Leon-Portilla, 1992).
- Friars called the codices "works of the devil" and framed burning them as religious duty (Leon-Portilla, 1992).
- Leon-Portilla argues destroying a knowledge system speeds cultural assimilation, so codex burning was inseparable from colonial domination, the same logic that suppressed Indigenous languages and banned ceremonies including pulque rituals (Leon-Portilla, 1992).

## Sources
- Leon-Portilla, M. (1992). Fifteen poets of the Aztec world. University of Oklahoma Press.
- Bruman, H. J. (2000). Alcohol in ancient Mexico. University of Utah Press.

module_title: Episode 12: Tequila & Mezcal — Heart of the Agave
title: From Sacred to Commodity
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can trace how a sacred plant became a colonial and then a global commodity.
Subject: Social Studies | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we talked about burning the books. Two quick questions.
One: roughly how many pre-Columbian codices survive today? [Beat] Fewer than 20, out of the thousands that existed before contact (Leon-Portilla, 1992).
Two: what did Leon-Portilla argue about destroying knowledge systems? [Beat] That destroying a knowledge system speeds cultural assimilation, so codex burning was inseparable from colonial domination (Leon-Portilla, 1992).

[HOOK] We have a plant that was sacred, with a goddess and a careful set of laws around it. Now watch what happens to it. Over a few centuries it goes from a holy fermented drink to a taxed colonial product, then to a symbol of a nation, and finally to a bottle sold all over the world. The plant did not change. What people did with it changed completely.

[TEACH] It starts with a new technology. When the Spanish arrived in 1521, they brought distillation, which the people here had not used on agave (Gaytan, 2014). Fermented pulque culture had been around for thousands of years. Now, in the colonial era running from 1521 into the 1700s, that same agave got distilled into something stronger: mezcal (Gaytan, 2014). A sacred fermented drink was turned into a distilled commercial product.
Then comes a contradiction worth noticing. The colonial authorities repeatedly banned mezcal, calling it a cultural and moral threat. But they also taxed it, because it brought in real revenue (Gaytan, 2014). So you have one hand trying to suppress the drink and the other hand reaching out to collect money from it. That tension, between suppression and extraction, runs right through the colonial period. They wanted it gone, and they wanted its income, at the same time.
Now the plant takes on a new meaning. During the Mexican War of Independence, from 1810 to 1821, agave spirits became a national symbol (Gaytan, 2014). Drinking the local spirit instead of imported European drink became a small act of identity, a way of saying this land and this product are ours. The sacred plant had become a flag.
Carry that forward to the modern arc. In 1974, Mexico established the tequila Denominacion de Origen, the legal designation that says only spirits made from blue Weber agave in designated Mexican regions can be called tequila (Gaytan, 2014). Read that as a postcolonial move. The same kind of trade law that colonial powers once used was now turned around, with Mexico asserting sovereignty over a product that had Indigenous roots and was now sold globally.
But sovereignty and pressure arrived together. In 1994, NAFTA opened the doors wider, and demand for tequila exploded internationally (Gaytan, 2014). That boom set off a tension we still feel: traditional production against industrial production. The faster and bigger the market wants tequila, the harder it pushes on the slow, small, traditional ways of making it.
So follow the whole line. Sacred fermented drink. Distilled colonial commodity. Banned and taxed at the same time. National symbol of independence. Protected postcolonial product. Global boom under NAFTA. One plant, carried through every one of those stages, its meaning rewritten at each step by whoever held the power.

[PICTURE] Picture a single agave heart passed down a long table of history. First a priest lifts it as something sacred. Then a Spanish official stamps it, bans it, and taxes it. Then a fighter for independence raises it like a flag. Then a lawyer in 1974 wraps it in a legal seal that says only Mexico may call this tequila. And finally a shipping container carries thousands of bottles of it across the world after 1994. Same heart, different hands, every hand giving it a new meaning.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: the Spanish brought distillation that turned fermented pulque culture into distilled mezcal in the colonial era of 1521 to the 1700s, colonial rule both banned and taxed it, agave spirits became a national symbol during the 1810 to 1821 War of Independence, and the 1974 tequila Denominacion de Origen asserted Mexican sovereignty over a product that NAFTA in 1994 pushed into a global demand explosion (Gaytan, 2014).

[CHECK] Quick check. What was the colonial contradiction in how authorities treated mezcal? [Beat] They repeatedly banned it as a cultural and moral threat while also taxing it for revenue, a tension between suppression and extraction (Gaytan, 2014).

## Key Takeaways
- The Spanish brought distillation in 1521, turning fermented pulque culture into distilled mezcal during the colonial era of 1521 to the 1700s (Gaytan, 2014).
- Colonial authorities repeatedly banned mezcal as a cultural and moral threat while taxing it for revenue, a tension between suppression and extraction (Gaytan, 2014).
- Agave spirits became a national symbol during the Mexican War of Independence, 1810 to 1821 (Gaytan, 2014).
- The 1974 tequila Denominacion de Origen was a postcolonial assertion of Mexican sovereignty over a now-global product, and NAFTA in 1994 set off a demand explosion and a traditional-versus-industrial tension (Gaytan, 2014).

## Sources
- Gaytan, M. S. (2014). Tequila! Distilling the spirit of Mexico. Stanford University Press.
- Leon-Portilla, M. (1992). Fifteen poets of the Aztec world. University of Oklahoma Press.

module_title: Episode 12: Tequila & Mezcal — Heart of the Agave
title: Geographic Protectionism
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can compare the geographic designation systems across Season 2 and ask who they really serve.
Subject: Economics | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time, in From Sacred to Commodity, we watched a drink change owners. Two quick questions.
One: what did the 1974 tequila Denominacion de Origen assert? [Beat] That tequila is a Mexican product, legally tied to designated regions and to the blue Weber agave (Gaytan, 2014).
Two: what was the colonial contradiction around mezcal? [Beat] Spanish authorities banned mezcal at times while still taxing it, treating the same drink as both forbidden and profitable (Gaytan, 2014).

[HOOK] All season we have watched law draw a fence around a place and say, only what grows inside this fence may carry this name. France did it. The United States did it. Martinique did it. Mexico did it twice. Four systems, one idea. But here is the question to sit with: who does that fence really protect, the heritage, or whoever drew the fence?

[TEACH] Let us line up all four Season 2 systems, because they share one move. Each one ties a product's economic value to a specific place through law.
France went first with the AOC in 1935, the system behind Champagne and Bordeaux, which fixes grape, method, and geography (Bowen, 2015). The United States wrote its bourbon standard in 1964, a national rule that says bourbon must be mostly corn, aged in new oak, and made in the United States. Martinique earned an AOC for its rhum agricole in 1996, requiring fresh cane juice and island production. And Mexico did it twice, with the tequila Denominacion de Origen in 1974 and the mezcal Denominacion de Origen in 1996, each tying the spirit to designated regions and traditional methods (Gaytan, 2014).
Same structure every time. A governing body, a boundary on the map, a list of requirements, and a name that only producers inside the fence may use. On its face, this protects culture and quality. It stops a factory in another country from slapping the word tequila on flavored grain spirit.
But here is the recurring key question across all four systems. Does geographic certification mainly protect cultural heritage and quality, or does it mainly create market advantages for whoever shapes the rules (Bowen, 2015)? Because someone writes those rules. Someone decides what counts as authentic, what method is allowed, what scale of producer can comply.
And the two Mexican systems show both edges of that blade. The tequila Denominacion de Origen is argued to favor large industrial producers, because its requirements and compliance costs are easier for big factories to meet than for small ones (Bowen, 2015). The mezcal Denominacion de Origen has the opposite criticism. Its certification process can create access barriers for small traditional producers, the very families who have made mezcal for generations, because registering and certifying costs money and paperwork that a village distiller may not have (Bowen, 2015; Gaytan, 2014). So a rule written to honor tradition can quietly push tradition out.

[PICTURE] Look at the tequila regulator in this lesson's panel, a figure in an official seat with a stamp and a map. Picture four fences drawn on four landscapes, France, Kentucky, Martinique, Jalisco. Each fence keeps imitators out. But look closer at who is standing comfortably inside each fence, and who is locked on the wrong side of a gate they cannot afford to open.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: all four Season 2 systems, the French AOC of 1935, the United States bourbon standard of 1964, the Martinique rhum agricole AOC of 1996, and Mexico's tequila Denominacion de Origen of 1974 and mezcal Denominacion de Origen of 1996, tie value to place through law, which raises one recurring question, whether geographic certification mainly protects heritage and quality or mainly creates market advantages for whoever shapes the rules (Bowen, 2015; Gaytan, 2014). The tequila system is argued to favor large producers, and the mezcal system can shut out small ones.

[CHECK] Quick check. What is the recurring key question that every Season 2 geographic designation system raises? [Beat] Whether geographic certification mainly protects cultural heritage and quality, or mainly creates market advantages for whoever shapes the rules (Bowen, 2015).

## Key Takeaways
- All four Season 2 systems tie economic value to a specific place through law: French AOC (1935), United States bourbon standard (1964), Martinique rhum agricole AOC (1996), and Mexico's tequila Denominacion de Origen (1974) and mezcal Denominacion de Origen (1996).
- The recurring question is whether certification mainly protects heritage and quality, or mainly creates market advantages for whoever shapes the rules (Bowen, 2015).
- The tequila Denominacion de Origen is argued to favor large industrial producers (Bowen, 2015).
- The mezcal Denominacion de Origen can create access barriers for small traditional producers (Bowen, 2015; Gaytan, 2014).

## Sources
- Bowen, S. (2015). Divided spirits: Tequila, mezcal, and the politics of production. University of California Press.
- Gaytan, M. S. (2014). Tequila! Distilling the spirit of Mexico. Stanford University Press.

module_title: Episode 12: Tequila & Mezcal — Heart of the Agave
title: The Jimador's Share
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain how value flows in tequila and why the harvester earns the least.
Subject: Economics | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time, in Geographic Protectionism, we lined up four legal fences. Two quick questions.
One: what is the key question all geographic designation systems raise? [Beat] Whether certification mainly protects heritage and quality, or mainly creates market advantages for whoever shapes the rules (Bowen, 2015).
Two: name one system and its year. [Beat] For example, the tequila Denominacion de Origen in 1974, or the French AOC in 1935 (Gaytan, 2014).

[HOOK] Take a sixty dollar bottle of premium tequila off the shelf. Inside it is sugar a man cut by hand from a plant heavier than he is, in the sun, for fifteen to twenty-five dollars a day. Follow where those sixty dollars actually go. The man who cut the agave gets almost none of it.

[TEACH] Let us walk the tequila supply chain for a sixty dollar premium bottle, stage by stage, and watch the money split.
At the very start is the agave farmer and the jimador, the harvester. The farming stage captures only about three to five percent of that retail price (Bowen, 2015). Production, the cooking, fermentation, and distillation, captures roughly eight to twelve percent. But the largest shares go to brand marketing, distribution, and retail, the parts of the chain farthest from the field. Brand development can take fifteen to twenty-five percent, distribution another fifteen to twenty percent, and the retail store the biggest slice of all.
Now zoom in on the jimador himself, the agave harvester. He earns roughly fifteen to twenty-five dollars a day swinging a sharp blade through heavy piñas (Bowen, 2015). His share of the retail value of the tequila made from the agave he cuts is well under one percent. He harvests the one ingredient without which there is no tequila, and the bottle returns him almost nothing.
Here is why that gap stays open, and it is the second hard fact: the agave price cycle. Blue Weber agave takes seven to eight years to mature (Bowen, 2015). So when demand rises and the price is high, everyone plants. But the new plants are not ready for seven or eight years. By the time that harvest arrives, the market is flooded, and the glut crashes prices. Jimadores and small farmers bear that boom-and-bust risk directly, because they do not hold the long-term contracts large producers use to lock in stable prices. This is the same commodity price volatility coffee farmers faced in Season 1, the people at the bottom carrying the swings.
And then comes the last twist, the celebrity premium. A celebrity tequila brand typically costs only about two to five dollars more to produce than a standard one, mostly for fancier packaging, but sells for fifteen to forty dollars more (Martineau, 2015). That extra price is almost entirely marketing and the famous name on the label, not better quality in the glass. The bottle that pays the harvester under one percent can charge you forty dollars extra for a story.

[PICTURE] Picture the sixty dollars as a stack of sixty coins on a long table. At the field end, the jimador reaches in and takes one coin, maybe not even a whole one. Slide down to the far end, past the trucks and warehouses, and the marketing team and the store scoop up handful after handful. The man who cut the plant is the smallest hand at the table.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: in a sixty dollar premium tequila, the agave farmer captures only about three to five percent and the jimador well under one percent of retail, while marketing, distribution, and retail take the largest shares (Bowen, 2015); the agave price cycle, where blue Weber takes seven to eight years to mature, drives boom-and-bust prices that jimadores and small farmers bear without contracts; and a celebrity tequila that costs two to five dollars more to make can sell for fifteen to forty dollars more, almost all of it marketing (Martineau, 2015).

[CHECK] Quick check. Roughly what share of a premium tequila bottle's retail value does the jimador capture, and why does the agave price cycle hurt him? [Beat] Well under one percent, and the cycle hurts him because blue Weber takes seven to eight years to mature, so planting in a boom leads to a later glut that crashes prices, a risk he bears without contracts (Bowen, 2015).

## Key Takeaways
- In a sixty dollar premium tequila, the agave farming stage captures only about three to five percent, while marketing, distribution, and retail capture the largest shares (Bowen, 2015).
- The jimador, the agave harvester, earns roughly fifteen to twenty-five dollars a day and captures well under one percent of the retail value (Bowen, 2015).
- The agave price cycle: blue Weber takes seven to eight years to mature, so demand-driven planting later floods the market and crashes prices, and small farmers bear that risk without contracts (Bowen, 2015).
- A celebrity tequila costs only about two to five dollars more to produce but sells for fifteen to forty dollars more, almost entirely from marketing (Martineau, 2015).

## Sources
- Bowen, S. (2015). Divided spirits: Tequila, mezcal, and the politics of production. University of California Press.
- Martineau, C. (2015). How the gringos stole tequila: The modern age of Mexico's most traditional spirit. Chicago Review Press.

module_title: Episode 12: Tequila & Mezcal — Heart of the Agave
title: Single Village Mezcal
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain how label transparency gives small mezcal producers more economic power.
Subject: Economics | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time, in The Jimador's Share, we followed the money inside a tequila bottle. Two quick questions.
One: roughly what share of a premium tequila bottle's retail value does the jimador capture? [Beat] Well under one percent (Bowen, 2015).
Two: what is the agave price cycle? [Beat] Because blue Weber takes seven to eight years to mature, planting during a demand boom leads to a later glut that crashes prices, a risk small farmers bear (Bowen, 2015).

[HOOK] In tequila, the harvester is often invisible, and the money flows away from the field. But there is a different model, and you can spot it on the label. Turn a bottle of artisanal mezcal around and it may tell you the exact village, the name of the man who distilled it, the species of agave, and the year it was harvested. That label is not just decoration. It is an economic tool.

[TEACH] Start with the contrast. Industrial tequila tends to hide the field. The location is often not disclosed, the agave is blue Weber and only blue Weber, the harvester is not named, and the producer's share of retail sits around five percent (Bowen, 2015). The value flows toward the brand and away from the person with the blade.
Now look at the artisanal, producer-owned mezcal model. Its centerpiece is the single village mezcal label, and that label names things. It names the exact village the mezcal comes from. It names the maestro mezcalero, the master distiller, the actual person. It names the agave species, which can be one of many, not just one. It names the harvest year. And it carries the NOM registration number, the official production registration (Bowen, 2015).
Here is the move that matters. All that naming works as an economic instrument. When a consumer can trace a bottle back to a specific person and a specific place, that specificity creates accountability. You are no longer buying a faceless brand story. You are buying this maestro's mezcal, from this village, this year. That traceability justifies a premium, and it can raise the producer's share of retail from roughly five percent toward fifteen to twenty-five percent (Bowen, 2015). The same dollar that mostly left the field in tequila now stays much closer to the hands that made the spirit.
And there is a second benefit that rides along with transparency. This direct-trade, cooperative approach can fold sustainability into the production culture itself (Bowen, 2015). When the producer is named and the buyer knows them, the relationship rewards long-term care of the land and the agave, rather than the fastest, cheapest harvest. Accountability for who made it tends to bring accountability for how it was made.
So the lesson from mezcal is not that small is automatically good. It is that information is power. A label that tells the truth about village, maker, species, and year shifts bargaining power back toward the producer, because the consumer now has someone real to trust and pay.

[PICTURE] Picture two bottles side by side. The first is a glossy tequila with a beautiful logo and no field behind it, the harvester nowhere on the label. The second is a plain mezcal bottle with handwriting on it, a village name, a person's name, an agave species, a year, and a registration number. The first sells a story. The second sells a person you can name, and the money lands much closer to his hands.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: a single village mezcal label names the exact village, the maestro mezcalero, the agave species, the harvest year, and the NOM registration number, and that transparency works as an economic instrument, because tracing a bottle to a specific person and place creates accountability and justifies a premium, raising the producer's share of retail from roughly five percent toward fifteen to twenty-five percent, while folding sustainability into the production culture (Bowen, 2015).

[CHECK] Quick check. How does a single village mezcal label increase a small producer's economic power? [Beat] By naming the exact village, maker, species, and year, it lets consumers trace the bottle to a specific person and place, which creates accountability, justifies a premium, and raises the producer's share of retail from about five percent toward fifteen to twenty-five percent (Bowen, 2015).

## Key Takeaways
- A single village mezcal label names the exact village, the maestro mezcalero, the agave species, the harvest year, and the NOM registration number (Bowen, 2015).
- That transparency is an economic instrument: tracing a bottle to a specific person and place creates accountability and justifies a premium (Bowen, 2015).
- It can raise the producer's share of retail from roughly five percent toward fifteen to twenty-five percent (Bowen, 2015).
- This direct-trade, cooperative approach can fold sustainability into the production culture itself (Bowen, 2015).

## Sources
- Bowen, S. (2015). Divided spirits: Tequila, mezcal, and the politics of production. University of California Press.

module_title: Episode 12: Tequila & Mezcal — Heart of the Agave
title: Reading the Codex
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can explain how to read an Aztec codex as a different kind of literature.
Subject: English Language Arts | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we looked at single village mezcal. Two quick questions.
One: what does a single village mezcal label name? [Beat] The producer, the village, and often the agave species, so you know exactly where the bottle came from.
Two: how does that transparency raise the producer's share? [Beat] When buyers can see the village and the maker, they pay a premium that channels more value back to the small producer instead of a distant company.

[HOOK] Every other episode this season, we read words. Persian wine poetry, Greek dialogue, Appalachian folk tales, all of it made of letters on a line. But the agave story was written down too, just not with an alphabet. It was painted. And to read it, you need a completely different set of skills.

[TEACH] Start with what a codex actually is. A codex is a Mesoamerican painted manuscript, made on deer hide or on bark paper called amate, and it is written in a complex visual language of pictographs and symbols (Leon-Portilla, 1992). These books recorded history, religion, astronomy, tribute, agricultural cycles, and myth. So a single codex could be art, history, data, and story all at once.
Now here is why reading one is so different from reading this sentence. Alphabetic text goes one way, left to right, top to bottom. A codex does not. Reading it takes three skills working together. First, spatial awareness, because the images run in many directions, and the path you follow is set by the narrative, not by the shape of the page. Second, iconographic knowledge, which means knowing what the symbols stand for. Footprints painted across a scene mean travel. A flame means burning. You have to learn the vocabulary of pictures before the page will speak. Third, contextual reading, where a single image works like a sentence and a cluster of images works like a paragraph, and the same picture can mean different things depending on what surrounds it.
The agave shows up in these books. A few codices survived, and one of them, the Codex Mendoza, made around 1541, holds tribute records showing communities paying maguey cloth and agave sap to the Aztec state (Leon-Portilla, 1992). So the plant we have followed all episode is literally written into the visual record of the people who first knew it.
Here is the literary point I want you to hold. After the conquest, Bishop Diego de Landa burned thousands of these texts and called them works of the devil and superstitions and lies (Leon-Portilla, 1992). Notice what that label does. Calling a knowledge system superstition is not a neutral description. It is a rhetorical move, and that move does political work. If you can convince people that another nation's records are just superstition, you make it easier to erase those records and the people who keep them. The word is a weapon.

[PICTURE] Look at the codex on this lesson. Do not try to read it like a paragraph. Let your eye wander. See the small painted figures, the footprints leading somewhere, the bundles of cloth stacked as tribute. Each picture is a word, each cluster a sentence. It is a whole library written in images, and most of its shelves were burned.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: a codex is a Mesoamerican painted manuscript on deer hide or bark paper, written in pictographs and symbols, and reading it takes spatial awareness, iconographic knowledge, and contextual reading rather than the left to right habit of alphabetic text (Leon-Portilla, 1992). It is not a lesser form of writing. It is a different one.

[CHECK] Quick check. What rhetorical work does it do when someone calls another culture's knowledge system superstition? [Beat] It frames that knowledge as worthless and false, which makes erasing it look justified instead of like the act of domination it really is (Leon-Portilla, 1992).

## Key Takeaways
- A codex is a Mesoamerican painted manuscript on deer hide or bark paper, written in a visual language of pictographs and symbols (Leon-Portilla, 1992).
- Reading one needs spatial awareness, iconographic knowledge, and contextual reading, not the one-direction flow of alphabetic text.
- Surviving codices such as the Codex Mendoza record agave, showing maguey cloth and agave sap paid as tribute (Leon-Portilla, 1992).
- Calling a knowledge system superstition, as Bishop Diego de Landa did, is a rhetorical move that does political work.

## Sources
- Leon-Portilla, M. (1992). Fifteen poets of the Aztec world. University of Oklahoma Press.

module_title: Episode 12: Tequila & Mezcal — Heart of the Agave
title: Selling the Sacred
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can analyze how agave spirit marketing uses Indigenous heritage, and explain what conscious consumption means.
Subject: English Language Arts | Run time: about 7 minutes

[RECALL] Last time we learned to read a codex. Two quick questions.
One: what skills does reading a codex require? [Beat] Spatial awareness, iconographic knowledge of the symbols, and contextual reading where images work like sentences and clusters like paragraphs (Leon-Portilla, 1992).
Two: what does calling a knowledge system superstition accomplish? [Beat] It frames that knowledge as worthless, which makes erasing it look justified rather than like an act of domination (Leon-Portilla, 1992).

[HOOK] Walk down the tequila aisle and you will see Aztec gods on the labels, words you cannot pronounce, photos of a worker swinging a blade in a sunlit field. The marketing is selling you heritage. The hard question is whose heritage it is, and who actually gets paid for it.

[TEACH] Modern tequila and mezcal marketing often invokes Indigenous heritage while serving corporate interests (Bowen, 2015). The playbook is consistent. Brands reach for Aztec and Mesoamerican imagery to claim ancient roots. They print phrases like traditional methods and family owned for generations to claim authenticity and continuity. They give products Indigenous-language names to claim a cultural connection. Each of these is a brand narrative, a story built to create value, and a critical reader treats it as a claim to be checked, not a fact to be swallowed.
So check it. A few verification questions cut straight through the imagery. Does the brand have any actual Indigenous ownership or partnership, or is the heritage just decoration? What does the NOM registration show, the official distillery number that tells you who really made it and how? Has the brand quietly been sold to a multinational, even while the label still says family owned (Bowen, 2015)? The image on the bottle is the marketing. The NOM number is the receipt.
Now the appropriation question, raised honestly and not as a lecture. When a multinational uses Mesoamerican art and images of jimadors to sell premium tequila, while the jimadors who do the brutal harvest work earn about 20 dollars a day, what is the nature of that representation (Bowen, 2015)? Honoring a culture and using its imagery to mark up a price are not the same act. That gap, between the sacred story on the label and the wage in the field, is the thing worth noticing.
Here is the part that keeps this from turning preachy. Whether a tequila tastes good and whether it was made justly are two separate questions, and you are allowed to hold both at the same time (Bowen, 2015). A spirit can be delicious and still come from an unfair chain. Pretending otherwise, in either direction, is just a simpler story than the truth. Holding both questions together is what we call conscious consumption. It does not tell you to drink or not to drink. It asks you to know what you are buying.

[PICTURE] Picture two images side by side. On the left, the bottle, polished, with a Nahuatl name and a painted god and the words family owned. On the right, the field, a jimador bent over a 200-pound agave heart in the heat, earning about 20 dollars for the day. Same product, two pictures. The label shows you one and hopes you never ask about the other.

[REMEMBER] The fact to carry out: agave spirit marketing often borrows Indigenous heritage, imagery, traditional-method claims, and Indigenous-language names, while serving corporate interests, and a critical reader verifies it by asking about real Indigenous ownership, the NOM registration, and whether the brand was sold to a multinational (Bowen, 2015). Conscious consumption means holding two separate questions at once: is it good, and is it just.

[CHECK] Quick check. Why are quality and justice separate questions when you evaluate a tequila? [Beat] Because a spirit can taste excellent and still be made through an unfair supply chain where the jimadors earn about 20 dollars a day, so the two questions have to be judged on their own and held together (Bowen, 2015).

## Key Takeaways
- Tequila and mezcal marketing often invokes Indigenous heritage while serving corporate interests, using Mesoamerican imagery, traditional-method claims, and Indigenous-language names (Bowen, 2015).
- A critical reader verifies the story by asking about actual Indigenous ownership, what the NOM registration shows, and whether the brand was sold to a multinational.
- The appropriation question is sharpest when a multinational sells premium tequila on the image of jimadors who earn about 20 dollars a day (Bowen, 2015).
- Conscious consumption means holding two separate questions at once: whether the spirit is good and whether it is made justly.

## Sources
- Bowen, S. (2015). Divided spirits: Tequila, mezcal, and the politics of production. University of California Press.

module_title: Episode 12: Tequila & Mezcal — Heart of the Agave
title: Key Terms: Tequila and Mezcal
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can define the core vocabulary from Episode 12 across all four lenses.
Subject: Vocabulary | Run time: about 5 minutes

Your word bank for the agave episode. Read it quick, out loud if you can. The full glossary lives in the course Resources.

## Geography

**Agave** (uh-GAH-vee). A desert succulent native to the Americas, with over 200 species, the source of tequila and mezcal (Valenzuela-Zapata & Nabhan, 2004).

**Blue Weber agave.** Agave tequilana, the only plant tequila may legally be made from, maturing in 6 to 8 years (Bowen, 2015).

**Espadin** (ess-pah-DEEN). The most cultivated mezcal agave, the workhorse species, maturing in 7 to 10 years.

**Tepeztate** (teh-pez-TAH-teh). A wild agave that can take 25 to 35 years to mature, pushed toward threat by demand (Torres et al., 2015).

## Social Studies

**Pulque** (POOL-keh). The fermented sap of the agave, sacred to the Aztecs and tied to the goddess Mayahuel (Bruman, 2000).

**Mayahuel** (mah-yah-WELL). The Aztec goddess of the agave and of pulque.

**Codex** (KOH-deks). A Mesoamerican painted book written in a visual language, most of which colonizers destroyed (Leon-Portilla, 1992).

**Denominacion de Origen.** A legal geographic designation, like the tequila DO of 1974, that ties a product to a place (Gaytan, 2014).

## Economics

**Jimador** (hee-mah-DOR). The skilled worker who harvests agave, earning about 15 to 25 dollars a day (Bowen, 2015).

**Pina** (PEEN-yah). The harvested heart of the agave, named for its pineapple shape, full of concentrated sugar.

**NOM.** The official Mexican registration number that lets buyers trace and verify a spirit's producer (Bowen, 2015).

**Maestro mezcalero** (mah-ESS-troh mez-kah-LEH-roh). The master mezcal distiller, keeper of traditional knowledge, named on single-village labels.

## English Language Arts

**Visual literacy.** The ability to interpret meaning from images, needed to read codices and marketing alike (Leon-Portilla, 1992).

**Iconography** (eye-kuh-NOG-ruh-fee). A symbolic visual language, like the codex symbols where footprints mean travel.

**Appropriation** (uh-proh-pree-AY-shun). Taking cultural elements without permission or understanding, as in some agave marketing (Bowen, 2015).

**Conscious consumption.** Holding both questions at once: whether a product is good and whether it is made justly.

## Sources
- Bowen, S. (2015). Divided spirits: Tequila, mezcal, and the politics of production. University of California Press.
- Bruman, H. J. (2000). Alcohol in ancient Mexico. University of Utah Press.
- Gaytan, M. S. (2014). Tequila! Distilling the spirit of Mexico. Stanford University Press.
- Leon-Portilla, M. (1992). Fifteen poets of the Aztec world. University of Oklahoma Press.
- Torres, I., Casas, A., Vega, E., Martinez-Ramos, M., & Delgado-Lemus, A. (2015). Population dynamics and sustainable management of mescal agaves in central Mexico. Economic Botany, 69(1), 26-41.
- Valenzuela-Zapata, A. G., & Nabhan, G. P. (2004). Tequila! A natural and cultural history. University of Arizona Press.

module_title: Episode 12: Tequila & Mezcal — Heart of the Agave
title: Cumulative Review: Tequila and Mezcal
text_content:
Goal: After this lesson you can recall the top facts from all four lenses and see how they connect.
Subject: Review | Run time: about 6 minutes

[RECALL] Let's pull the agave episode together, one quick fact per lens, then the thread that ties them.

[TEACH] Start with geography. Agave is a stress specialist, the most extreme terroir in the season, making richer sugar the harder its life (Valenzuela-Zapata & Nabhan, 2004). The same blue Weber agave makes floral tequila in highland Los Altos and earthier tequila in the lowland Tequila Valley (Bowen, 2015). And agave thinks in decades, so harvesting slow wild species like Tepeztate before they flower pushes them toward extinction (Torres et al., 2015).

Now social studies. Agave was sacred long before it was a commodity. Pulque was tied to the goddess Mayahuel and governed by detailed Aztec law (Bruman, 2000). Spanish colonizers burned the codices, the painted books, leaving fewer than 20 pre-Columbian survivors, a deliberate erasure of a knowledge system (Leon-Portilla, 1992). And the sacred plant became a colonial and then global commodity, with the 1974 tequila Denominacion de Origen asserting Mexican sovereignty (Gaytan, 2014).

Then economics. Geographic designation systems all raise the same question, do they protect heritage or favor the powerful (Bowen, 2015). In tequila, the jimador who harvests the agave earns about 15 to 25 dollars a day and captures under 1 percent of retail, while celebrity branding adds huge markups (Martineau, 2015). But single-village mezcal labels, naming the village, maestro, and species, hand small producers more bargaining power (Bowen, 2015).

Last, language. A codex is visual literature, read through space, color, and symbol, and calling it superstition was a political act (Leon-Portilla, 1992). And modern marketing sells Indigenous heritage while the jimadors earn little, which is why conscious consumption holds quality and justice as separate questions (Bowen, 2015).

[REMEMBER] Here is the thread. Agave turns hardship into sweetness, and its spirits carry a sacred history that was nearly erased and is now sold back to the world. Geography shows the stress that makes the flavor, history shows the culture and its erasure, economics shows who profits and who harvests, and language shows the difference between honoring a heritage and appropriating it.

[CHECK] One last check before the quiz. Why is agave the season's most extreme example of terroir? [Beat] Because for agave, stress is not just helpful but the entire point, it concentrates richer sugar the harder its conditions are (Valenzuela-Zapata & Nabhan, 2004).

## Key Takeaways
- Geography: agave is a stress specialist, the same plant makes different tequila by elevation, and slow wild species face extinction (Valenzuela-Zapata & Nabhan, 2004; Torres et al., 2015).
- Social studies: agave was sacred to the Aztecs, the codices were burned, and a sacred plant became a sovereign commodity (Bruman, 2000; Leon-Portilla, 1992; Gaytan, 2014).
- Economics: designation systems raise hard questions, jimadors earn the least, and single-village transparency helps small makers (Bowen, 2015; Martineau, 2015).
- Language: codices are visual literature, and marketing that sells heritage calls for conscious consumption (Leon-Portilla, 1992; Bowen, 2015).

## Sources
- Bowen, S. (2015). Divided spirits. University of California Press.
- Bruman, H. J. (2000). Alcohol in ancient Mexico. University of Utah Press.
- Gaytan, M. S. (2014). Tequila! Distilling the spirit of Mexico. Stanford University Press.
- Leon-Portilla, M. (1992). Fifteen poets of the Aztec world. University of Oklahoma Press.
- Martineau, C. (2015). How the gringos stole tequila. Chicago Review Press.
- Torres, I., Casas, A., Vega, E., Martinez-Ramos, M., & Delgado-Lemus, A. (2015). Population dynamics and sustainable management of mescal agaves in central Mexico. Economic Botany, 69(1), 26-41.
- Valenzuela-Zapata, A. G., & Nabhan, G. P. (2004). Tequila! A natural and cultural history. University of Arizona Press.

title: Sources and Further Reading: Tequila and Mezcal
text_content:
Every claim in this episode is cited inline so you can check it yourself. Here is the full reference list in APA style. Use it to verify a fact, to dig deeper, or to start your own research for the project. The episode is educational, respectful of Indigenous culture, and not an endorsement of drinking.

## References

Bruman, H. J. (2000). Alcohol in ancient Mexico. University of Utah Press.

Gaytan, M. S. (2014). Tequila! Distilling the spirit of Mexico. Stanford University Press.
