module_title: Episode 12: Tequila & Mezcal — Heart of the Agave
title: Knowledge Check: Tequila and Mezcal
text_content:
This check pulls from all four lenses in Episode 12: geography, social studies, economics, and language.

Each time you take it, you get 10 questions drawn from a larger pool, and the answer choices are shuffled, so a retake is never the same test twice. You need 80 percent to pass, and you can retake it as many times as you like. The goal is understanding, not gatekeeping.

After each question you see an explanation and a citation. Read them. The reasons a wrong answer is wrong are the best study material here.

When you finish, take the Project assignment for this episode, where the bigger questions, how stress makes flavor and how a sacred plant became a global commodity, get the long-form treatment they deserve.
quiz_content: {"passingScore":80,"attemptsAllowed":-1,"questionsPerAttempt":10,"shuffleOptions":true,"questions":[{"id":"q1","questionText":"How is agave different from grain, grape, and cane in what it needs?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q1a","text":"It thrives on hardship, poor soil, drought, and heat, which makes its sugar richer"},{"id":"q1b","text":"It needs rich soil and constant water"},{"id":"q1c","text":"It only grows in cold climates"},{"id":"q1d","text":"It needs no sunlight"}],"correctOptionId":"q1a","explanation":"Unlike crops that want comfort, agave is a stress specialist: the harder its conditions within its range, the more concentrated and complex its sugar becomes.","citation":"Lesson: The Stress Specialist"},{"id":"q2","questionText":"Why does stress make agave better for spirits?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q2a","text":"Stress makes the plant concentrate denser, more complex sugar in its core, which becomes the spirit"},{"id":"q2b","text":"Stress kills the plant"},{"id":"q2c","text":"Stress adds water"},{"id":"q2d","text":"Stress has no effect"}],"correctOptionId":"q2a","explanation":"A stressed plant slows down and concentrates resources, packing more sugar into its heart to survive, and that sugar is what distillers turn into spirit.","citation":"Lesson: The Stress Specialist"},{"id":"q3","questionText":"Why does highland Jalisco tequila taste different from lowland tequila?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q3a","text":"Highland agave grows higher, cooler, and more stressed, giving floral, sweet tequila; lowland gives earthier, herbal tequila"},{"id":"q3b","text":"They use different plants"},{"id":"q3c","text":"Lowland tequila is not real tequila"},{"id":"q3d","text":"There is no difference"}],"correctOptionId":"q3a","explanation":"The same blue Weber agave grown high and cool in Los Altos turns floral and sweet, while the warmer Tequila Valley lowland makes it earthier and herbal.","citation":"Lesson: Highland and Lowland"},{"id":"q4","questionText":"What plant is tequila legally required to be made from?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q4a","text":"Blue Weber agave, in designated regions centered on Jalisco"},{"id":"q4b","text":"Any cactus"},{"id":"q4c","text":"Sugarcane"},{"id":"q4d","text":"Corn"}],"correctOptionId":"q4a","explanation":"Tequila's Denominacion de Origen limits it to blue Weber agave grown in designated regions, with Jalisco at the center.","citation":"Lesson: Highland and Lowland"},{"id":"q5","questionText":"Why does harvesting wild agave threaten the species?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q5a","text":"Agave only makes seeds when it flowers, but it is harvested just before flowering, so it cannot reproduce"},{"id":"q5b","text":"Harvesting helps it spread"},{"id":"q5c","text":"Wild agave never reproduces anyway"},{"id":"q5d","text":"It does not threaten the species"}],"correctOptionId":"q5a","explanation":"Because agave is cut just before it flowers, when its sugar peaks, harvesting usually stops it from seeding, so cutting it faster than it reproduces makes the species decline.","citation":"Lesson: Agave and Time"},{"id":"q6","questionText":"Roughly how long can a wild Tepeztate agave take to mature?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q6a","text":"About 25 to 35 years"},{"id":"q6b","text":"About 6 months"},{"id":"q6c","text":"About 2 years"},{"id":"q6d","text":"It matures instantly"}],"correctOptionId":"q6a","explanation":"Tepeztate can take 25 to 35 years to mature, far longer than blue Weber's 6 to 8 years, which is why overharvesting it is so risky.","citation":"Lesson: Agave and Time"},{"id":"q7","questionText":"Which Mexican state makes about 85 percent of all mezcal?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q7a","text":"Oaxaca, with its extreme microclimate and agave-species diversity"},{"id":"q7b","text":"Jalisco"},{"id":"q7c","text":"Texas"},{"id":"q7d","text":"Yucatan"}],"correctOptionId":"q7a","explanation":"Oaxaca produces about 85 percent of mezcal, helped by its extreme microclimate diversity and many agave species.","citation":"Lesson: Highland and Lowland"},{"id":"q8","questionText":"What was pulque to the Aztecs?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q8a","text":"A sacred fermented agave drink, tied to the goddess Mayahuel and tightly regulated"},{"id":"q8b","text":"A common everyday water"},{"id":"q8c","text":"A distilled spirit"},{"id":"q8d","text":"A type of food"}],"correctOptionId":"q8a","explanation":"Pulque, the fermented sap of the agave, was sacred to the Aztecs, associated with the goddess Mayahuel, and governed by detailed law.","citation":"Lesson: The Sacred Plant"},{"id":"q9","questionText":"How did Aztec law regulate pulque?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q9a","text":"By social role and context, setting who could drink and when, with harsh penalties for public drunkenness"},{"id":"q9b","text":"By banning it for everyone"},{"id":"q9c","text":"By allowing unlimited use"},{"id":"q9d","text":"It had no rules"}],"correctOptionId":"q9a","explanation":"Aztec law allowed pulque for elders, nursing mothers, warriors after battle, priests in ritual, and the general public only at festivals, a system based on role and context, not blanket rules.","citation":"Lesson: The Sacred Plant"},{"id":"q10","questionText":"What were Mesoamerican codices?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q10a","text":"Painted books in a sophisticated visual language recording history, religion, astronomy, and daily life"},{"id":"q10b","text":"Stone weapons"},{"id":"q10c","text":"Spanish tax forms"},{"id":"q10d","text":"Agave farming tools"}],"correctOptionId":"q10a","explanation":"Codices were painted manuscripts on deer hide or bark paper, written in a complex visual language distinct from European alphabets.","citation":"Lesson: Burning the Books"},{"id":"q11","questionText":"What happened to most Mesoamerican codices?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q11a","text":"Thousands were destroyed by colonizers, and fewer than 20 pre-Columbian examples survive"},{"id":"q11b","text":"They are all preserved in museums"},{"id":"q11c","text":"They were never written"},{"id":"q11d","text":"They were sold to Mexico"}],"correctOptionId":"q11a","explanation":"Thousands of codices existed before contact, but fewer than 20 pre-Columbian examples survive, with major burnings like Diego de Landa's 1562 auto-da-fe in Yucatan.","citation":"Lesson: Burning the Books"},{"id":"q12","questionText":"Why does Miguel Leon-Portilla argue codex burning was inseparable from colonial domination?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q12a","text":"Destroying a knowledge system accelerates cultural assimilation, so it served colonial control"},{"id":"q12b","text":"The books were worthless"},{"id":"q12c","text":"It was an accident"},{"id":"q12d","text":"It had no cultural effect"}],"correctOptionId":"q12a","explanation":"Leon-Portilla argued that knowledge systems sustain cultural identity, so destroying them accelerates assimilation, the same logic that suppressed Indigenous languages and ceremonies.","citation":"Lesson: Burning the Books"},{"id":"q13","questionText":"How did a sacred plant become a colonial commodity?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q13a","text":"The Spanish brought distillation, turning fermented pulque culture into distilled mezcal that was banned and taxed"},{"id":"q13b","text":"The Aztecs sold it to Europe"},{"id":"q13c","text":"It was never commercialized"},{"id":"q13d","text":"It became illegal forever"}],"correctOptionId":"q13a","explanation":"Spanish distillation turned agave into mezcal, which colonial authorities both banned as a cultural threat and taxed for revenue, a contradiction of suppression and extraction.","citation":"Lesson: From Sacred to Commodity"},{"id":"q14","questionText":"What did the 1974 tequila Denominacion de Origen represent?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q14a","text":"A postcolonial assertion of Mexican sovereignty over a now-global product"},{"id":"q14b","text":"A French law"},{"id":"q14c","text":"A ban on tequila"},{"id":"q14d","text":"A new agave species"}],"correctOptionId":"q14a","explanation":"The 1974 DO let Mexico claim legal control over tequila as a global product, a postcolonial sovereignty tool, though its fairness to small producers is debated.","citation":"Lesson: From Sacred to Commodity"},{"id":"q15","questionText":"What key question do all geographic designation systems raise?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q15a","text":"Do they mainly protect heritage and quality, or mainly help whoever shapes the rules?"},{"id":"q15b","text":"Do they make the product cheaper?"},{"id":"q15c","text":"Do they change the climate?"},{"id":"q15d","text":"Do they ban all imports?"}],"correctOptionId":"q15a","explanation":"From the French AOC to the tequila DO, each system raises the same question: whether certification protects culture and quality, or mostly advantages those who write the rules.","citation":"Lesson: Geographic Protectionism"},{"id":"q16","questionText":"About what share of a 60 dollar premium tequila does the agave farmer capture?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q16a","text":"About 3 to 5 percent"},{"id":"q16b","text":"About 60 percent"},{"id":"q16c","text":"About 95 percent"},{"id":"q16d","text":"Exactly half"}],"correctOptionId":"q16a","explanation":"The agave farmer captures only about 3 to 5 percent, while brand marketing, distribution, and retail take the largest shares.","citation":"Lesson: The Jimador's Share"},{"id":"q17","questionText":"What is the agave price cycle?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q17a","text":"Blue Weber takes 7 to 8 years to mature, so a planting rush during high demand floods the market years later and crashes prices"},{"id":"q17b","text":"Agave prices never change"},{"id":"q17c","text":"Agave is harvested daily"},{"id":"q17d","text":"Prices only ever rise"}],"correctOptionId":"q17a","explanation":"Because blue Weber takes 7 to 8 years to grow, everyone plants when demand is high, then the harvest arrives years later into a glut that crashes prices, and small farmers bear the risk.","citation":"Lesson: The Jimador's Share"},{"id":"q18","questionText":"What does the celebrity tequila premium illustrate?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q18a","text":"A bottle that costs only 2 to 5 dollars more to make can sell for 15 to 40 dollars more from marketing, not quality"},{"id":"q18b","text":"Celebrities make better tequila"},{"id":"q18c","text":"Premium tequila costs nothing to make"},{"id":"q18d","text":"Marketing has no effect on price"}],"correctOptionId":"q18a","explanation":"Celebrity brands cost about 2 to 5 dollars more to produce but sell for 15 to 40 dollars more, a premium driven almost entirely by marketing and association, not production quality.","citation":"Lesson: The Jimador's Share"},{"id":"q19","questionText":"What does a 'single village mezcal' label name?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q19a","text":"The exact village, the maestro mezcalero, the agave species, the harvest year, and the NOM number"},{"id":"q19b","text":"Only the price"},{"id":"q19c","text":"Nothing specific"},{"id":"q19d","text":"Only the country"}],"correctOptionId":"q19a","explanation":"Single-village mezcal labels name the village, the master distiller, the agave species, the harvest year, and the registration number, making the product fully traceable.","citation":"Lesson: Single Village Mezcal"},{"id":"q20","questionText":"How does single-village label transparency help the producer economically?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q20a","text":"Traceability creates accountability and justifies a premium, raising the producer's share from about 5 percent toward 15 to 25 percent"},{"id":"q20b","text":"It lowers the price"},{"id":"q20c","text":"It has no economic effect"},{"id":"q20d","text":"It removes the producer's name"}],"correctOptionId":"q20a","explanation":"When consumers can trace a bottle to a specific person and place, that specificity creates accountability and supports a premium, lifting the producer's share of retail.","citation":"Lesson: Single Village Mezcal"},{"id":"q21","questionText":"How does reading a codex differ from reading alphabetic text?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q21a","text":"It needs spatial awareness and knowledge of symbols, since images run in many directions and work like sentences"},{"id":"q21b","text":"It is read exactly like a novel"},{"id":"q21c","text":"It has no meaning"},{"id":"q21d","text":"It uses only letters"}],"correctOptionId":"q21a","explanation":"Codex reading requires spatial awareness, iconographic knowledge (footprints mean travel, a flame means burning), and contextual reading where single images act as sentences.","citation":"Lesson: Reading the Codex"},{"id":"q22","questionText":"What rhetorical work does calling a knowledge system 'superstition' do?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q22a","text":"It frames destruction as a religious duty and serves political purposes"},{"id":"q22b","text":"It praises the knowledge"},{"id":"q22c","text":"It has no effect"},{"id":"q22d","text":"It is purely scientific"}],"correctOptionId":"q22a","explanation":"Bishop Diego de Landa called the codices 'works of the devil,' a framing that recast destroying a rival knowledge system as righteous duty, doing political work.","citation":"Lesson: Reading the Codex"},{"id":"q23","questionText":"What does agave-spirit marketing often do with Indigenous heritage?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q23a","text":"It invokes Mesoamerican imagery and 'tradition' to sell premium products that may serve corporate interests"},{"id":"q23b","text":"It always credits Indigenous communities fairly"},{"id":"q23c","text":"It avoids all imagery"},{"id":"q23d","text":"It has no connection to heritage"}],"correctOptionId":"q23a","explanation":"Marketing frequently uses Aztec imagery, claims of tradition and family ownership, and Indigenous-language names, which a critical reader should verify against ownership and NOM records.","citation":"Lesson: Selling the Sacred"},{"id":"q24","questionText":"What does conscious consumption mean in this episode?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q24a","text":"Holding both questions at once: whether a spirit tastes good and whether it is made justly"},{"id":"q24b","text":"Only caring about taste"},{"id":"q24c","text":"Never drinking anything"},{"id":"q24d","text":"Ignoring how a product is made"}],"correctOptionId":"q24a","explanation":"Conscious consumption treats quality and justice as separable but equally real questions, worth holding at the same time.","citation":"Lesson: Selling the Sacred"},{"id":"q25","questionText":"Why is agave the most extreme example of terroir in the season?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q25a","text":"For wine and whiskey a little stress helped, but for agave stress is the entire point"},{"id":"q25b","text":"Agave ignores its environment"},{"id":"q25c","text":"Agave grows anywhere identically"},{"id":"q25d","text":"Agave has no flavor"}],"correctOptionId":"q25a","explanation":"Where other crops tolerate some stress, agave depends on it, concentrating sugar through hardship, so it is the most extreme expression of terroir all season.","citation":"Lesson: The Stress Specialist"},{"id":"q26","questionText":"How far back does the Mexican relationship with agave reach?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q26a","text":"Cultivation evidence reaches back about 10,000 years, one of Mexico's oldest agricultural relationships"},{"id":"q26b","text":"Only to the 1900s"},{"id":"q26c","text":"Only since NAFTA"},{"id":"q26d","text":"It is brand new"}],"correctOptionId":"q26a","explanation":"Evidence of agave cultivation reaches back about 10,000 years, making it one of Mexico's oldest agricultural relationships.","citation":"Lesson: The Sacred Plant"},{"id":"q27","questionText":"What is a jimador?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q27a","text":"The skilled worker who harvests the agave, earning roughly 15 to 25 dollars a day"},{"id":"q27b","text":"A brand of tequila"},{"id":"q27c","text":"A type of agave"},{"id":"q27d","text":"A government official"}],"correctOptionId":"q27a","explanation":"A jimador is the skilled agave harvester, who earns about 15 to 25 dollars a day and captures well under 1 percent of the tequila's retail value.","citation":"Lesson: The Jimador's Share"},{"id":"q28","questionText":"What does NOM on a mezcal label refer to?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q28a","text":"The official Mexican registration number that helps trace and verify the producer"},{"id":"q28b","text":"The price in pesos"},{"id":"q28c","text":"The alcohol percentage"},{"id":"q28d","text":"The bottle size"}],"correctOptionId":"q28a","explanation":"The NOM is the Norma Oficial Mexicana registration number, a transparency and accountability mechanism that lets buyers verify who made a spirit.","citation":"Lesson: Single Village Mezcal"},{"id":"q29","questionText":"How does Aztec pulque regulation compare to modern alcohol law?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q29a","text":"It was more differentiated, governing use by social role and context rather than blanket bans or permission"},{"id":"q29b","text":"It was identical to modern law"},{"id":"q29c","text":"It had no rules at all"},{"id":"q29d","text":"It banned all drinking forever"}],"correctOptionId":"q29a","explanation":"Aztec law tied pulque use to social role, occasion, and purpose, a more differentiated system than most modern blanket alcohol regulation.","citation":"Lesson: The Sacred Plant"},{"id":"q30","questionText":"What can a codex's visual format communicate that alphabetic text cannot?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q30a","text":"Meaning carried through spatial arrangement, color, and symbol all at once"},{"id":"q30b","text":"Nothing; it is just decoration"},{"id":"q30c","text":"Only sounds"},{"id":"q30d","text":"Only numbers"}],"correctOptionId":"q30a","explanation":"A codex packs meaning into the spatial layout, colors, and symbols of an image, conveying relationships at a glance that a line of letters cannot.","citation":"Lesson: Reading the Codex"},{"id":"q31","questionText":"Why is the tequila DO criticized?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q31a","text":"It is argued to favor large industrial producers, while the mezcal DO can block small traditional ones"},{"id":"q31b","text":"It makes tequila free"},{"id":"q31c","text":"It bans Jalisco"},{"id":"q31d","text":"It has no critics"}],"correctOptionId":"q31a","explanation":"Critics argue the tequila DO advantages industrial producers, and the mezcal DO creates access barriers for small traditional makers, raising the question of who the rules serve.","citation":"Lesson: Geographic Protectionism"},{"id":"q32","questionText":"What is a maestro mezcalero?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q32a","text":"A master mezcal distiller and keeper of traditional knowledge, often named on the bottle"},{"id":"q32b","text":"A tequila tax collector"},{"id":"q32c","text":"A type of still"},{"id":"q32d","text":"A wild agave"}],"correctOptionId":"q32a","explanation":"A maestro mezcalero is the master distiller who carries the traditional knowledge of mezcal making, and single-village labels name this person directly.","citation":"Lesson: Single Village Mezcal"},{"id":"q33","questionText":"What kind of soil does agave grow best in?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q33a","text":"Volcanic, well-drained soil that is low in organic matter"},{"id":"q33b","text":"Rich, wet, fertile soil"},{"id":"q33c","text":"Pure sand with no minerals"},{"id":"q33d","text":"Frozen ground"}],"correctOptionId":"q33a","explanation":"Agave grows best high up in volcanic, well-drained, low-nutrient soil, conditions that stress the plant and concentrate its sugar.","citation":"Lesson: The Stress Specialist"},{"id":"q34","questionText":"How does agave's value distribution compare to Season 1's coffee?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q34a","text":"Both leave the grower with a tiny share while most value goes to brands, distribution, and retail"},{"id":"q34b","text":"Agave farmers get all the money"},{"id":"q34c","text":"They are completely different"},{"id":"q34d","text":"Coffee farmers earn more than brands"}],"correctOptionId":"q34a","explanation":"Like Ethiopian coffee farmers, agave growers capture only a small share of retail, with most value flowing to brands, distributors, and retailers in wealthy markets.","citation":"Lesson: The Jimador's Share"},{"id":"q35","questionText":"What is appropriation in agave-spirit marketing?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q35a","text":"A corporation using Indigenous art and images while the actual Indigenous workers earn very little"},{"id":"q35b","text":"Crediting and paying Indigenous communities fairly"},{"id":"q35c","text":"Avoiding all cultural references"},{"id":"q35d","text":"A type of distillation"}],"correctOptionId":"q35a","explanation":"Appropriation here means a multinational using Mesoamerican motifs and images of jimadors to sell premium spirits while those jimadors earn about 20 dollars a day.","citation":"Lesson: Selling the Sacred"},{"id":"q36","questionText":"What is the ecological cost of unsustainable wild-agave mezcal?","questionType":"multiple_choice","options":[{"id":"q36a","text":"Demand pushes slow-growing wild species like Tepeztate toward being threatened or extinct"},{"id":"q36b","text":"There is no cost"},{"id":"q36c","text":"It improves the soil"},{"id":"q36d","text":"It makes agave grow faster"}],"correctOptionId":"q36a","explanation":"Because wild agave takes decades and is harvested before it can seed, rising demand pushes species like Tepeztate toward being threatened, the direct cost of unsustainable production.","citation":"Lesson: Agave and Time"}]}
